sentences.text: Yes.
So actually she's the only one that I knew.
And my brother, Leon, knew another young person that also-- sometimes we got together.
But there was no way of socializing.
There were only two things to do in our lives, survive and work.
That's, that's, that's all it was.
And my escape was only reading.
At a very early age, I told you that I was introduced to a book, and I never stopped reading.
In my teens, I started to read Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Balzac.
You name it, I read them.
I was in a different world.
That was my only escape and going out with Leon.
I-- we went out together to a movie.
And if it's-- it was not for me, then I-- if I was too young, I used to make believe I'm older.
That was the only thing that I really had, going to a movie.
Sometimes maybe we went to a cafe.
I don't remember.
But that was all.
There was no fun.
There was no-- there was-- we were not young.
I was an old person when I was 11.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had no childhood.
I had no childhood.
I had no youth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, let me go now-- 1944.
June 6, 1944, I come home from work, yeah, I was very undernourished.
I had boils on my legs.
I had frostbites on my feet.
I had to wear my father's shoes to go to work.
I could not wear-- I only had one pair of shoes, couldn't wear them anymore.
All right.
I was really-- in 1944, I was skin and bones, but so were my brothers.
Leon worked for a boulangerie-- baker.
And he delivered bread with a wagon and a dog underneath.
But in the bakery, he could eat all he wanted.
So he always had enough bread.
He couldn't take out any bread.
The bread he delivered was counted.
One day he came home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the train from outside of Brussels, somebody left a valise.
And he came home.
And what do you think was in the valise?
A bottle of milk, a bread, butter, and eggs.
We had a feast.
I remember that meal.
That was the only meal in the years that was substantial, that was fabulous, fabulous, the only time.
OK, let's go back to June 6, 1944.
It's in the evening.
We're home where we lived illegally.
I had my own little room.
The doorbell rings.
I go downstairs-- it was a walk-up, the first floor.
I run downstairs the step.
I opened the door-- the outside door.
A man stands there.
And he asks me in French if a certain person lived here, a name I'd never heard.
I said, no.
He said, oh.
And he left.
OK, thank you.
I go upstairs, I said, Papa, I don't like it.
Let's get out of here.
He said, Sonja, what's the matter with you?
You're seeing things?
I said, Papa, I don't want to stay.
Let's leave.
Something is wrong.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father dissuaded me that-- and nothing was wrong.
At that time, he was working.
We were making cigarettes, filling paper-- tobacco and making the cigarettes and sell them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told you I was reading a lot.
That night, it was 2 o'clock in the morning.
I had always very little sleep.
But I needed to read to escape my real world.
I was reading.
And I hear the boots coming up.
And I am petrified.
I know what is happening.
I am immobilized.
I can hardly breathe.
I haven't slept yet.
And the Nazis came up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a man that was looking for Jews.
And he gets paid per head for the Jews that he finds.
And he found us.
And he was looking whether we were home.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they come in, three of them.
And I'm in my room.
And I hear my father talking outside.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go out-- get out of bed, and I stand in the middle of my room.
The door is closed.
My father comes in.
And the German comes in.
And I'm standing in the middle, and I'm paralyzed.
I cannot move.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my father begs him, please, go out, let my daughter get dressed.
You seen what condition she's in.
He decided to walk out of my room.
My father had to help me dress.
I couldn't even dress myself.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I remember the remark that, that one German said-- made.
We had a map on the wall, and with little flags, we were following the front line.
And I remember him saying, I don't understand.
The Jews are not allowed to have radios, he said in German.
But they all know where the front line is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, the three of us were taken.
Our apartment was sealed.
We were put into prison, into a prison, common prison.
Where I was, was like a holding cell, holding pen.
And there I met other Jewish people.
From there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were transported to a place called Melin.
Melin is a place that is a army, army-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] where the army lives, army barracks that are for the Belgians, which, of course, wasn't used because there was no Belgian Army.
This was situated between Brussels and Antwerp.
That's where the Jews were accumulated until there were enough to ship to Auschwitz.
Pardon me?
Papa, Blanca, and myself.
We were shipped to Melin from the prison.
And there were multitudes of people.
We were in barracks, sleeping there, like, on, on cots, on floors.
I don't know myself.
But when I came in, I had a little gold ring that I had outgrown.
I could never get it off.
And they forced me to take it off.
They forced me.
And I remember the struggle to take off that little gold ring.
My finger was swelling.
And I started to cry.
I couldn't get it off.
Finally, I spit on it and spit on it, finally got it off.
I was all swollen.
And I was thinking to myself, a little gold ring.
How many gold rings are they accumulating from the Jewish people?
This is just a little gold ring.
They take away everything we have.
They don't let us live.
And why?
What do they want of us?
What have we done?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So anyway, we were there in the barracks.
That was June 7th during the night that this happened.
I'll never forget the sound of the boots as long as I live, as long as I live.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were there from June 7th or 8th.
I don't know when they transported out.
But I was taken away June 7th-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until there were enough people accumulated and then we were shipped to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There, the people that went with me became my friends.
They were my friends.
I was the youngest.
I was 17 and 1/2 years old then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were pushed into the cattle cars and it was closed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were like sardines in there-- no air.
There was a big can for urine.
I, I don't know.
But there was-- I suddenly start to understand.
Now, I have to go back.
In 1943, my brother, Norbert, was caught.
He was 19 years old.
He was brought to Melin.
But he was smart.
He had a crowbar on him.
And he had $25-- 25 franc sewn in his jacket in the back because he was not going to go there.
And when he was in the cattle car in 1943, a year before me, he pried open the cattle bar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he jumped out while riding in the car.
But he had to jump out in Belgium yet when it was dusk, not too light.
Because once he's out of Belgium, he's finished.
He cannot be-- go to Poland.
They shot after him.
They grazed his head.
But they did not-- he rode-- he waited until he rode over, over a ravine, and then he walked until he went to a-- came to a farmhouse.
And he told them who he was.
And he had 25 franc he took out.
And he took a train back to Brussels.
Very few people came back from that transport.
When I was in that cattle car, I suddenly understood what my brother did.
For the life of me, I could not see how he could have done it.
I felt I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a place where there was no escape, no escape.
Even if I would have had a crowbar, I would not have been able to do-- and good for him.
He would never have survived.
He was too weak and sick, but we-- he had so much guts.
Wait till you hear my, my, my brother Leon's stories.
He had a lot of guts, too.
In other words, when it depends on your life, you become resourceful.
You find extra energy.
But only the realization came to me when I was in the cattle car what actually happened.
Therefore, I want to bring something out.
Nobody that wasn't there can really understand the Holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only-- we only talk about it.
You can understand-- you can-- everybody can understand what they're saying.
But it is really incomprehensible.
The only thing that shows the truth are the pictures that were taken by the liberation, by the-- by the-- by the liberation armies that took the pictures directly in the concentration camps.
Those were the true pictures.
Everything else is just imitation.
This is-- are the true pictures.
Because only when I was in the cattle car did I understand what my brother did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were like cattles in there.
It is really made for cattles.
We had one big problem in there, in that car.
There was a pregnant woman.
She was gaunt.
She must have been in her sixth month easily.
But she was losing her mind.
She started to fantasize-- and losing her mind.
And my-- Blanca tended to her.
And she always said to her, Blanca, [SPEAKING  GERMAN], Blanca, save me.
Save me.
And it was so awful.
That trip to Auschwitz was so awful.
The horror had begun.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrive in Auschwitz.
I don't remember how long that trip took.
I have no idea.
It seemed forever.
It was a stench.
It was hot.
It was June.
There was no air.
We come out, out of the car.
We are told to get in line in two.
And I, I hear there are-- people that go to the left go with trucks into the camp.
People that go to the right will go-- walk into the camps.
And Blanca and I walked up.
And there stands the man that-- I didn't know who he was at that time, a high German officer, Mengele.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we arrive, when I and Blanca came in front of him, he goes to me like this [POINTS] and to her like this.
[POINTS]
I stood at attention.
I said, in German, excuse me, Herr Kommandant, this is my mother.
Is it possible that we stay together?
And he looked at me.
And he went like this.
[POINTS]
He could have sent us both this way.
I did not know.
Those people went straight to the chambers, gas chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] actually, in Brussels, in '44, we had already heard that they're killing Jews in the concentration camps.
It already came through.
It was known.
It was known.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was known.
But still, I was very young.
I could not really-- I did not really want to believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, we entered Auschwitz-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] horror, horror, horror.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The barracks, the barracks, the barrenness-- those wooden, enormous barracks with nothing but wooden planks to sleep on.
Hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people in one of those barracks.
I don't know if it were hundreds.
Maybe it were 500, maybe it were a thousand.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The stench-- it was summer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those big barrels for urine for the night.
The people dehumanized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- I said, I can't believe.
We went-- we went-- we were stripped of everything we were wearing.
We were taken.
Our heads were shaved off.
We were going into showers.
There was a huge barrack.
And water-- cold water ran down on everybody.
And then I got a dress.
And I walked out wet-- and something on my feet.
And that was it.
And we went to the barracks.
And the next thing we were told-- to stand outside Appell, hours at an end in the heat.
And I don't know what happened to me.
I felt very, very weak.
And suddenly, somebody slaps me in the face.
And I catch myself.
And the woman next to me says, Sonja, you should be ashamed of yourself!
You're the youngest in our group.
Will you pull yourself together?
I said, why?
What's the matter?
I was about to faint.
I was keeling over-- the heat and the sun.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, I don't-- I don't know what to do.
She said, you pull yourself together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want you to think that you are making it.
I said, OK.
OK.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so went one day after another, standing there for many hours, the depravity to smell the burning incinerators, the burning flesh, the chimneys smoking, the cadavers that were lying around, the people being picked up in, in carriages and pulled by, by inmates-- inmates, our own people, inmates.
They were skeletons.
They were bones-- skin and bones with their eyes glazed over.
They were barely alive.
They were taking them into the incinerators.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How can that go on?
[CRYING] I wish I wouldn't have come.
[SOBBING] This is too painful.
[CRYING] The summer passed.
It became September.
The starvation was terrible.
The degradation was terrible.
[SOBBING] People were beaten with whips and dogs.
And [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was never hit, except once.
We went past information.
We went past the guard.
And inside the guardhouse, I saw a map with flags of the front.
And I made one step.
[SOBBING] All I wanted to see-- where the front was.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the guard came out [CRYING] and hit me so hard that I nearly fell to the floor.
[CRYING] But that was the only time.
[CRYING] It became colder and colder.
This-- and now it was Yom Kippur.
And I don't fast.
I don't do any of those things anymore.
But I decided to fast completely.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[SOBBING] And I fasted that if there is a God, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let them-- let them help us.
But there can't be a God.
[CRYING] How can He let this happen?
[SOBBING] What have we-- what have we done in our lives, except be alive-- barely?
So [CRYING] I refused the tiny bit of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
I became very-- I remember once I saw the masses of people.
You are not a human being.
You're just-- you're just like an animal lying there, a neglected animal that's not even being fed, that has no, no-- [CRYING] that has absolutely no needs.
We went to a bathroom.
There was one barrack, holes inside, holes.
And I looked.
If this wouldn't have been so tragic, it was almost-- I almost had to laugh.
But it was too tragic.
Rows and rows of holes in wooden encasements.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Once I saw in Auschwitz-- I saw two potato peels.
I ran like crazy to get to two potato peels.
But somebody got it before me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It got so cold with no clothes on.
And the people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stopped being people.
They, they were weak.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- many couldn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could barely fight.
The poor woman-- for some reason, that woman that was pregnant-- I don't know how she made it-- but she went on my side.
And she went into the camp.
And she laid down on the floor.
And she kept turning her head.
And we went over to her, Blanca and I.
And she said, Blanca, save me.
Blanca, save me.
They finally picked her and took care of her.
She, too, was incinerated.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was standing there, Appell.
And I was seeing small sparrows flying in and out of the camp, in and out of the electrified fences.
And I looked at them.
I said, you are mocking us.
You are small birds.
You're free.
You are flying out.
I said, give me your wings.
[SOBBING] Let me fly out.
Let me be free.
I said, I want out of here.
[SOBBING] Arbeit macht frei.
That's the entrance to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] I saw one woman running to the electrified gate and committing suicide.
That was rarely done.
People tried to survive, unless it becomes so bad that they're so indifferent, lethargic, and that-- they just take them away, burned, literally incinerated.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It went into October.
I was freezing.
I was weak.
We are told we are going to leave Auschwitz end of October.
We are put into those showers again, cold showers, in that huge, enormous barrack.
We take a cold shower.
I get a dress, maybe panty, and some, some shoes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over the wet, cold shower, and out in October, out into the open in Auschwitz, in Poland-- freezing cold wind.
Huh?
OK.
